content.full_text:
November 19, 1975

Robert H. Cornell, Chairman  
California Commission on  
Uniform State Laws  
2150 Aetna Building  
Crocker Plaza  
San Francisco, Calif. 94104

Re: Request for Opinion  
No. 75-197

Dear Mr. Cornell:

Thank you for your letter of November 4, 1975, requesting an opinion concerning the duty of the California Commission on Uniform State Laws to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act.

Pursuant to Section 87300, "every agency" must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code. However, it would not further the purposes of the Act to require agencies which have no "designated employees" who would be subject to such a code to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code. Accordingly, the PPPC staff will draft a proposed regulation which will enable agencies which do not have "designated employees" to satisfy the requirement of Section 87300 by filing some sort of letter or form in lieu of a complete Conflict of Interest Code.

Treatment of the question you have raised by means of a regulation obviates the need to issue an opinion in response to your request. Therefore, no formal opinion will be issued. I will, however, keep you informed with respect to the proposed regulation which will resolve your problem.

If you wish to request a review of the denial of your request for a formal opinion, you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. If you have any questions, please contact Kenneth Finney, Director of the Legal Division.

Sincerely,

Daniel H. Lowenstein  
Chairman, for the Commission

This is a letter from the California Commission on Uniform State Laws dated November 4, 1975, addressed to the Fair Political Practices Commission. The letter is signed by Robert H. Cornell, Chairman of the Commission on Uniform State Laws.

The letter requests an opinion from the Fair Political Practices Commission regarding whether the Commission on Uniform State Laws is required to adopt and promulgate a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to the provisions of the Political Reform Act of 1974.

The Commission notes that the organization and duties of the commission are contained in Chapter 3 (commencing with Section 10400), Part 2, Division 2, Title 2 of the Government Code. It states that the commission apparently meets all of the criteria established by the Fair Political Practices Commission in its administrative definition of a "state agency" (Sec. 18249, 2 Cal. Adm. C.). However, members of the commission probably do not qualify as "designated employees," which must be subject to a Conflict of Interest Code since they are unsalaried and perform essentially advisory functions.

The letter concludes with the signature of Robert H. Cornell and is signed "Very truly yours."

cc: All Commissioners, CCUSL
